This man superintended the casks and cisterns; he also amused himself with carpentering. Now he possessed several pairs of pincers, among which was one both big and heavy. I then, thinking it would suit my purpose, took it and hid it in my straw mattress.

questions.text: When someone claims Cellini might escape, how does he respond to such accusations?

questions.text: How does Cellini handle being used for someone else's emotional or psychological needs?
questions.held_out_passage: At times the poor man detained me for four or five stricken hours without ever letting me cease talking. He used to keep me at his table, eating opposite to him, and never stopped chatting and making me chat; but during those discourses I contrived to make a good meal. He, poor man, could neither eat nor sleep; so that at last he wore me out.

questions.text: When given an opportunity to boast about his abilities, what does Cellini claim he can accomplish?
questions.held_out_passage: I answered that it had always been my ambition to do those things which offer the greatest difficulties to men, and that I had done them; as to flying, the God of Nature had gifted me with a body well suited for running and leaping far beyond the common average, and that with the talents I possessed for manual art I felt sure I had the courage to try flying.

questions.text: How does Cellini approach problem-solving when planning an escape?

questions.text: How would Cellini characteristically handle the logistics and challenges of following a royal court?
questions.held_out_passage: At this time, however, we were following the court with the weariest trouble and fatigue; the reason of this was that the train of the King drags itself along with never less than 12,000 horse behind it; this calculation is the very lowest; for when the court is complete in times of peace, there are some 18,000, which makes 12,000 less than the average. Consequently we had to journey after it through places where sometimes there were scarcely two houses to be found; and then we set up canvas tents like gipsies, and suffered at times very great discomfort.

questions.text: When Cellini's workers report on his accomplishments to a rival or authority figure, what pattern emerges in how the story gets told versus what actually happened?
questions.held_out_passage: In answer to his questions, the two men whom I suspected of having caked my metal for me, said I was no man, but of a certainty some powerful devil, since I had accomplished what no craft of the art could do; indeed they did not believe a mere ordinary fiend could work such miracles as I in other ways had shown. They exaggerated the whole affair so much, possibly in order to excuse their own part in it, that the majordomo wrote an account to the Duke, who was then in Pisa, far more marvellous and full of thrilling incidents than what they had narrated.

questions.text: What were the birth and death years of Benvenuto Cellini?
Who translated this autobiography of Benvenuto Cellini?
What time period does Cellini's autobiography cover?
In which two countries was the scene of Cellini's autobiography mainly set?
Which famous artist did Cellini know and adore?
What were three of Cellini's professions or skills mentioned in the text?
According to the introductory note, which three autobiographies are mentioned as masterpieces that compete with Cellini's for first place in its class?
What major historical event is described in Cellini's autobiography because he personally fought in it?
To whom did Cellini dictate his autobiography?
What was the age of the person to whom Cellini dictated his autobiography?
Why might Cellini's motivation for writing his autobiography have made it more likely to contain both accurate observations and self-serving distortions compared to Augustine's or Pepys's works?

Given that Cellini lived during the Reformation and Counter-Reformation but focused mainly on artistic rather than religious or political matters, what does this suggest about his priorities and the scope of his influence?
How does the fact that Cellini was 'a murderer and a braggart' yet also 'a great master of narrative' complicate the reliability of his autobiography as a historical document?

What does the introductory sonnet's reference to 'precious time in vanity I've spent' suggest about Cellini's perspective on his life when writing, and how might this have influenced what he chose to emphasize in his autobiography?

Why is it significant that Cellini dictated his autobiography to a fourteen-year-old boy rather than writing it himself, considering what the text says about his character and his desire to control his legacy?
How does Cellini's belief that 'all men of merit' should write their own life stories reflect both his confidence in his own achievements and his understanding of how history would judge him?

Given that Cellini knew Michelangelo and was himself 'no inconsiderable figure' among Renaissance artists, what does the text suggest about the competitive artistic environment of sixteenth-century Italy?

Why would Cellini's participation in the Sack of Rome be more likely to appear in his autobiography than other major historical events of his era, based on what the text reveals about his narrative priorities?

How do Cellini's character flaws—being 'insolent, sensual, inordinately proud and passionate'—potentially explain both his artistic achievements and his tendency toward 'misrepresentations' and 'suppressions of damaging facts'?

What does the text's observation that Cellini would be remembered 'less by his Perseus and his goldsmith's work than by the book' suggest about the relationship between artistic creation and historical documentation?

How does the period covered by the autobiography (1500-1562) relate to Cellini's statement in the sonnet about 'precious time in vanity I've spent,' and what might this reveal about his self-assessment?
What would Benvenuto Cellini's opinion be on 3D metal printing technology and its impact on traditional goldsmithing?
If Cellini were alive today, which contemporary art movement would he most likely align himself with?
What did Cellini think about the artistic merit of Japanese metalworking techniques?
How would Cellini have responded to the invention of the photograph and its effect on representational sculpture?
What were Cellini's views on the proper treatment of animals in his personal life beyond his documented hunting activities?
If Cellini had access to modern chemistry, which synthetic materials would he have chosen to incorporate into his designs?
What did Cellini believe about the existence of life on other planets or celestial bodies?
How would Cellini have rated his own artistic legacy compared to artists who emerged after his death?
What were Cellini's private thoughts on the role of women as artists and patrons in Renaissance society?
If Cellini could commission a work from any artist in history, who would he choose and why?

Cellini claims to write for posterity to document his merit, yet he admits to suppressions and misrepresentations. How does he reconcile his stated commitment to being 'a man of truth' with his acknowledged selective presentation of facts?

He expresses remorse in the introductory sonnet about time 'spent in vanity,' yet his autobiography extensively celebrates his sensual exploits and violent acts. Is this remorse genuine, or merely a rhetorical gesture expected of aging men?

Cellini was simultaneously a murderer and a master craftsman devoted to beauty. Did he see these aspects of himself as fundamentally separate, or did he believe his artistic genius somehow justified or transcended his violent nature?

He dictated his life story 'casually to a lad of fourteen while he went about his work,' suggesting it was not a carefully considered project. How much of his self-presentation should we attribute to spontaneous honesty versus unrehearsed self-deception?

The text notes he had 'no manner of doubt' about his merit. Given his acknowledged pride and passion for proving himself right, how would Cellini have responded to genuine criticism of his work or character?

He describes himself as insolent and inordinately proud, yet also as devoted to serving patrons like the Pope and King of France. Could he genuinely submit to authority, or was his service merely transactional performance?

Cellini lived through the Reformation and Counter-Reformation but largely ignored these epochal events in his autobiography. Does this omission reflect his actual indifference to religious and political upheaval, or a deliberate choice to center himself?

He 'adored' Michelangelo yet positioned himself as 'no inconsiderable figure' among great artists. Did Cellini genuinely believe himself Michelangelo's peer, or was this competitive self-assessment a defensive posture masking insecurity?

The autobiography emphasizes his 'passionate enthusiasm for the creation of beauty,' yet he also engaged in casual violence and licentiousness. Could these impulses coexist in his psychology as equally authentic, or did he compartmentalize them?

Cellini's greatest legacy became his narrative skill rather than his 'Perseus' sculpture, a fact he could not have anticipated. If he had known this, would it have fundamentally altered his self-conception as an artist, or would he have rationalized it as proof of his genius?
